summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the common barrier that prevents people from blogging: fear of writing itself. He acknowledges that writing is genuinely difficult and requires real effort to master. However, he argues that this difficulty is precisely why hesitant writers should blog anyway—writing regularly is exercise that inevitably improves skill. He recommends treating writing as an extension of natural conversation and offers practical resources for non-writers looking to improve.
key_points: Writing is hard and can't be faked or bought—it requires genuine work to improve
Regular blogging serves as exercise that will inevitably make you a better writer
Avoiding writing due to intimidation creates a self-perpetuating cycle of never improving
Good writing should sound natural, like spoken conversation rather than 'writerly' prose
Fear of writing shouldn't prevent people from blogging if they have something to say
money_quotes: Write a small blog entry a few times every week and you're bound to become a better writer.
If you're not writing because you're intimidated by writing, well, you're likely to stay that way forever.
Consider your writing a natural extension of your spoken conversations.
If it sounds like writing, I rewrite it.
Don't let fear of writing keep you from doing it.
key_insight: The intimidation that keeps people from writing is the very reason they should write regularly—avoidance guarantees stagnation while practice guarantees improvement.
